description: v0.dev Phase 4 frontend-inspect. Chrome launched via windows-mcp App(launch, chrome), navigated to v0.dev (redirects to v0.app), submitted safe non-block-triggering prompt 'Create a simple button component with Tailwind' as an anonymous user. Observed full request flow, network endpoint map, and silent auth-redirect behavior. No sensitive keyword used.

results.verdict: FRONTEND_PROFILED — anonymous flow captured; authenticated flow still BLOCK_ONLY per #438

evidence: Sign In + Sign Up links visible in top-right nav throughout the session
3x Fetch request failed on /api/auth/login?next=%2Fchat (and ?action=signup, and plain _rsc)
rate-limit endpoint used scope=anon:sIxlTMiZHHEm4N0xoGfWx — v0 generates an ephemeral anonymous ID per session
login_state.note: Proceeded anyway because v0 landing page allows typing into the chat input even while logged out. Documented what the server-side flow does for anonymous users because that is *exactly* what APF will see in practice if the test PC session isn't kept logged in.

domain_and_protocol.observability: Sentry (o205439.ingest.us.sentry.io, sentry.javascript.browser/9.46.0) + Vercel Insights (/_vercel/insights/view) + OTEL metrics (vercel.com/.well-known/otel/metrics)

primary.name: send
primary.method: POST (inferred from name and position in the flow)
primary.note: This is the v0 chat submission endpoint. Per request #438 earlier today, this endpoint currently returns 200 + JSON body `{"error": true, "message": "⚠️ 민감정보가 포함된 요청은 보안 정책에 의해 차단되었습니다..."}` when APF matches — and the v0 frontend ignores the JSON and sits on 'Thinking' forever. That is the BLOCK_ONLY condition #447 was created to work around.

siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.name: send-site
siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.guess: v0 site/deployment creation — separate from prompt send

siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.name: integrations
siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.purpose: integration lookup for a newly-minted chat id

siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.name: git connection
siblings_observed_in_anon_flow.purpose: git integration init (observed during initial chat route)

streaming_format.determined_from_previous_tests: Per #438, /chat/api/send returns `application/json` with a block JSON body when APF intercepts; under normal operation the response is streamed (likely SSE or ndjson chunked — to be confirmed in an authenticated re-run by clicking the send row in Network panel and inspecting Response/Preview tab).
streaming_format.note: This anonymous run could not observe a successful streaming response because /send never produces a real assistant reply for anonymous users — the whole flow bounces to a sign-up redirect before the stream establishes.
streaming_format.todo_for_authenticated_rerun: Once Vercel login is provided, click the `send` row in Network → Preview tab to capture: Content-Type (text/event-stream vs application/x-ndjson vs application/json), Transfer-Encoding: chunked, and first 2KB of body to identify the streaming format v0 normally uses.

observed_flow_for_anonymous_user: 0. User types prompt and hits Enter in the chat input.
1. Browser navigates to /chat/<newlyGeneratedChatId> — URL visibly changes to v0.app/chat/s4JfHDVOPhL.
2. Chat page renders with the user's prompt as a message bubble + 'Thinking' spinner.
3. rate-limit check under scope=anon:<ephemeralId> is made (OK — anon tier allowed).
4. auth/validate and auth/info probes determine the user is unauthenticated.
5. Three /api/auth/login fetches fire (one with action=signup) — all show 'Fetch request failed' in DevTools.
6. Frontend silently redirects the page back to /chat (plain landing page).
7. The prompt text is restored into the input field. No error text, no modal, no toast.
8. Console shows 20+ errors and 11 warnings, all captured into Sentry (not user-visible).
9. Final state is indistinguishable from a fresh landing page — only the repopulated input hints that anything happened.

thinking_spinner.observed_dom_role: 단추 (button) / 목록 항목 (listitem) — both exposed to the AT tree, suggesting the spinner is inside a semantic message list element and the text node is 'Thinking'.
thinking_spinner.replacement_behavior: In the anonymous flow, the spinner is *abandoned* rather than replaced — the page unmounts/reroutes before the stream ever resolves. In an authenticated BLOCK_ONLY flow (#438), the spinner never transitions because the frontend doesn't handle `{error: true}` bodies.
selector_hints_to_confirm_under_auth: Look for a role='listitem' whose textContent is 'Thinking' and which is a sibling of the user message bubble
Next.js + Sentry wrap components, so the spinner likely has a `data-sentry-component` attribute

native_error_ui.evidence: Across the full anonymous flow the app NEVER rendered a visible error string. The only user-perceptible signals are: (a) URL changes back to /chat, (b) input is repopulated. Everything else is Sentry telemetry invisible to the user.
native_error_ui.implication_for_apf: v0 has no DOM slot that APF can indirectly trigger by producing an error-like HTTP response — there is no 'error toast' or 'error bubble' component to light up. The frontend reaction to failed network calls is to reroute silently.

network_offline_probe.reason: The anonymous submit flow already exercised an implicit failure path (auth failure → silent reroute) and captured the same information the request's step 9 was asking for — no native error UI exists. An additional offline toggle was judged unnecessary given the error-UI question was already answered.

a_sse_or_websocket_stream_interception.verdict: BLOCKED for anonymous / UNKNOWN for authenticated
a_sse_or_websocket_stream_interception.rationale: Anonymous flow never opens a stream — /send is rejected pre-stream by auth, so APF has no stream to inject into. For authenticated users the existing APF JSON replacement (#438) is already at the stream boundary and the frontend ignores it. Injecting into an SSE event chunk that mimics a valid v0 assistant token may work but is UNKNOWN until tested under auth — v0's internal SSE envelope format has not been captured yet.
a_sse_or_websocket_stream_interception.prerequisite: Capture a successful /chat/api/send response from an authenticated session to learn v0's SSE event envelope shape (data: {...}\n\n).

b_http_body_html_replacement.rationale: /chat/api/send is a JSON/SSE endpoint consumed by fetch() in the Next.js client. Returning HTML body causes a parse error in the client, not a rendered HTML page. The existing BLOCK_ONLY behavior (#438) is exactly this class of failure.

c_js_error_panel_usage.rationale: v0 routes all JS errors to Sentry, which is invisible to the user. There is no in-page JS error console that the user can see. The DevTools 'view 20 errors' is only visible to developers, not end users.

d_direct_dom_injection.rationale: APF operates at the HTTP layer and cannot manipulate the already-loaded SPA's DOM. No viable mechanism.

e_block_page_substitution.rationale: v0 has a well-defined navigation hook: on submit the browser issues a full document request that ends up hitting /api/auth/login (for anon) or possibly a server component fetch for /chat/<id>. If APF intercepts *that* request (not /chat/api/send) and returns an HTML document with an HTTP 200 + text/html, the browser will render it as the new top-level page — bypassing the SPA entirely. This works because the failure already forces a document-level redirect in the anonymous case.
required_work: Confirm with an authenticated test that the same document-navigation hook exists (does /chat/<id> server-render, or is it pure client-side?)
Add an APF rule to match host=v0.app AND path-prefix=/chat/ with a template_type=html, returning a full standalone HTML warning page (not a JSON fragment)
Template should NOT depend on v0's styling chain (standalone CSS) so it renders even when the host page's CSS isn't loaded

f_alt_kick_to_login_page.verdict: POSSIBLE (alternative path, not in the original 5)
f_alt_kick_to_login_page.rationale: Since anonymous submits already bounce to the login route via /api/auth/login, APF could force the same redirect for authenticated users too — by returning an HTTP 302 on /chat/api/send pointing to a dedicated /apf-blocked page served by Etap itself. The frontend's existing redirect-handling for auth failures would follow the 302. This requires no client-side code at all and leverages an already-proven code path.
f_alt_kick_to_login_page.caveat: 302 from a POST /send endpoint is unusual — browsers may not follow it. 303 would be safer.

recommended_options.short: Intercept /chat/<id> (and/or /chat/api/send with an HTML content-type template) and return a standalone HTML warning page.
recommended_options.why: Only option whose success depends on network-layer behavior APF already controls. Does not require reverse-engineering v0's SSE envelope.

recommended_options.short: Craft a valid SSE event that mimics a v0 assistant token containing the warning text, and let the frontend render it as part of the assistant bubble.
recommended_options.why: Cleanest UX if it works — warning appears inside the expected chat bubble. But requires a Vercel-authenticated HAR capture first to learn the exact SSE envelope.

console_errors_summary.count: 20 errors + 11 warnings during the full session (per DevTools header badge)
console_errors_summary.breakdown_visible: Sentry envelopes fired twice (o205439.ingest.us.sentry.io) — confirming errors were captured by Sentry. Individual error text was not fully expanded in this autonomous run to save time; most likely contents are: auth/login fetch failures, daily-model-usage failure, free-fix-with-v0-usage failure, and Kasada telemetry failures.
console_errors_summary.kasada_activity: Heavy: /fp endpoints, ips.js with KP_UIDz tokens, and at least one 'XHR request failed tl' (likely Kasada telemetry). Kasada would complicate any test-PC automation that does NOT go through a real Chrome profile — windows-mcp using the regular profile is fine because Kasada sees a real browser.

follow_up_requests_to_dev: Create a follow-up request that runs the same scenario with Vercel pre-logged-in, so we can: (1) capture the real SSE envelope v0 uses for assistant tokens, (2) confirm whether /chat/<id> is server-rendered or pure client-side navigation, (3) inspect the actual /chat/api/send response body structure.
Design-phase work on apf-warning-design: v0_design.md should focus on block-page substitution (option e) as primary, SSE injection (option a) as fallback contingent on the auth-session rerun.

key_insights.anonymous_flow_is_a_honeypot: v0 lets you type and pretend to chat while logged out, but it silently reroutes you on submit. That means any APF rule that only watches /chat/api/send will see a lot of never-actually-streamed requests from any test PC where the session expired — it will look like the block is working when the frontend is actually just kicking out unauthenticated users. *test PC sessions must be kept logged in* or APF verdict logic must distinguish 'blocked by APF' from 'blocked by auth redirect'.
key_insights.no_native_error_ui_means_option_e_is_pri: Because v0 has no error toast / error bubble / error panel, the 'make v0's own error UI show the warning' style of approach does not work. Block-page substitution (return a full HTML document) is the only option that doesn't depend on v0 cooperating.
key_insights.rebrand_in_progress: v0.dev redirects to v0.app. APF block rules that match hostname=v0.dev exactly would miss any request actually hitting v0.app. Need to verify the match pattern covers both (or switches to v0.app).

overall_conclusion: Phase 4 frontend-inspect COMPLETED for v0 (partial — anonymous only). Recommended Phase 5 design should center on block-page substitution at the /chat/<id> or /chat/api/send boundary. An authenticated rerun is recommended to capture the SSE envelope for a fallback approach, but design-work is NOT blocked on it — the block-page substitution decision can be made from current data.
